answer:
We test the argument by assuming the premises true and the conclusion false.  
If the conclusion ~I is false, then I is true.  
With I true, the first premise H ≡ (~I ∨ J) becomes H ≡ (false ∨ J), so H ≡ J.  
The second premise H ∨ ~J must be true. If H and J are both false, H ≡ J holds but H ∨ ~J is false (since ~J is true, actually false? Wait: if H false and J false, ~J true, so H ∨ ~J is true—checking).  
Actually, if I true, H false, J false: first premise false ≡ (false ∨ false) → false ≡ false, true; second premise false ∨ true = true; conclusion ~I false. This works as a counterexample.  

That matches option G: Invalid. Counterexample when I is true and H and J are false.

\boxed{G}
